Learn Python From a JavaScript Dev's Angle (1) - Table of Contents and Rambling

This is my third iThome Ironman challenge this year, and on top of that it's my fourth Ironman series overall. Honestly, I'm a little scared I won't finish this one, because the series I'm tackling this time is basically learning from scratch — and learning it from a JavaScript angle on top of that. So all I can really do is hope I make it to the finish line.
Learning Python From a JavaScript Angle

The main point of this series is to push myself to keep growing and learning. On top of that, I just wanted to pick up another programming language, and getting to know more languages helps me understand what makes each one tick. That's why I decided to give Python a shot. And whenever I learn a new language, I have this habit of starting from the basics — this series is no exception.
The one thing that makes this series a little special is that this time I want to try learning Python through a

lens. So throughout the series you'll still see a lot of JavaScript syntax, and even side-by-side comparisons of the two. The idea is to make Python feel a bit more familiar to frontend devs who want to learn it.
So why Python and not, say, PHP? Pretty simple — I've already

Even before I got into frontend, I kept hearing people around me say Python is super easy to learn. And I saw people using it for web scraping and data analysis all the time. But the thing is, if you try to write a scraper without a basic understanding of frontend, you'll run into all kinds of tricky spots and gotchas. As it happens, I've been writing some scrapers lately to pull data — I used Node.js at the time — but I still wanted to try doing it in a different language. Building things in another language is a great way to understand its quirks and strengths.

So this series is going to start from the basics of Python. The early chapters will lean heavily on beginner-level fundamentals, and where it makes sense, I'll compare the basic syntax against JavaScript.
I figure... that's probably easier to grasp and absorb for a JavaScript dev who wants to get into Python (probably, anyway).
I've rambled enough for one post, so let's get into "
Oh, by the way — you're going to see a lot of memes in this series. The main reason is I want to keep things light and easy to read for whoever's following along. For example...
Wife, come out and watch God with the Bull Demon King
Anyway, I've already warned you that you're in for a pile of memes, so don't be surprised when they show up.

Some of you might want to jump straight into learning Python, but no matter what I'm doing, I like to do things properly from start to finish. So the first three posts or so (including this intro) will lean more toward chatting about installing Python and that kind of thing.
I'll see you in the next post for the rest.
One last reminder: this series mainly uses Python 3. I might touch on Python 2 here and there, but only in passing, so the focus is firmly on Python 3.
